narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 36 and plans to attend full-time, you've put together a thoughtful application for law school, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 and background from a lower-middle income family. The admissions model we use predicted a 67% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools rely on this to prioritize students likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or character, but a data-driven assessment based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic records, family backgrounds, and exam outcomes. The three biggest factors driving this decision were your Year 3 school grade decile, your LSAT performance, and your Year 1 school grade decile, ranked by their influence on the model's thinking for your specific profile. Most critically, your low Year 3 decile—placing you in the bottom 10% of your school—strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure, as students in higher deciles typically show the sustained performance needed to master the intense legal material. Your LSAT score, while close to the average of 36.7, worked in your favor by pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing, reflecting its role as a key indicator of analytical skills for the bar. Your Year 1 decile, also in the bottom 10% compared to the typical mid-range of around the 5th or 6th decile, added further weight toward the failure prediction, highlighting early academic challenges that often persist. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your deciles are notably lower, while your LSAT holds up well, but those early and later grade positions stood out as the dominant concerns. This isn't set in stone—many applicants with similar starts have improved dramatically through targeted prep. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your deciles, perhaps through a post-baccalaureate or master's in a related field, and aim to push your LSAT higher if possible. Building a stronger academic track record now could flip the script for your next application, showing the upward trajectory the model values. You're clearly motivated, and with focused effort on those grade deciles, law school doors could open wider soon.
